figures.caption: Figure 3 Visualization of HCC token triage. Green tokens (retained) capture high-frequency structures, red tokens (evicted) fall on featureless regions, while orange tokens (merged) crucially preserve soft gradients and continuous surfaces that would be permanently lost under pure eviction baselines.
Table 1 3D reconstruction on 7-Scenes [45] (200 frames). Best and second-best results are highlighted. OOM indicates out-of-memory failure.
Figure 2 Qualitative comparison with StreamVGGT, Evict3R, and InfiniteVGGT on 7-Scenes and NRGBD. Our method yields more complete, sharper, and less noisy reconstructions
Table 3 Quantitative comparison on ETH3D (Outdoor) dataset. Ours200 and Ours400 denote results with cache budgets of 200K and 400K tokens, respectively.
Table 2 3D reconstruction on NRGBD [48] across two sequence lengths. Best and second-best results are highlighted.

Figure 1 Overall architecture of our streaming 3D reconstruction framework. To bound the KV cache under an O(1) memory budget, we introduce a robust cache management pipeline. Raw activation scores are first denoised by the Cross-Layer Consistency Scoring module. Based on these refined scores, the Hybrid Cache Compression (HCC) module executes a three-tier triage: highly salient tokens are RETAINed, redundant tokens are EVICTed, and moderately important tokens are MERGEd (absorbed into retained anchors via nearest-neighbor assignment), preserving critical geometric context without exceeding memory constraints.
Table 4 Video depth evaluation on Bonn [50] and KITTI [51] across different sequence lengths. OOM indicates out-of-memory.
Table 5 Sensitivity analysis of CLCES hyperparameters on 7-Scenes at 300 frames.
